cards.back: Range of frequencies where |H(ω)| ≥ 1/√2 (≈ 0.707) of peak. Also called half-power bandwidth.
20log₁₀(1/√2) = −3.01 dB. At this point, power is halved (P ∝ V², V drops by 1/√2 → P drops by 1/2).
Frequencies where |H(ω)| ≥ 1/√2. Filter passes signal with minimal attenuation.
Frequencies where |H(ω)| is 40-50 dB below peak. Signal effectively blocked (attenuated to ~1%).
Range between passband edge and stopband edge. Always finite width in causal filters. Narrower = better but → more ripple or higher order.
More ripple in passband (for same filter order). Or: need higher order (more complexity) for same ripple.
Sharper transition, but more components, more implementation difficulty, and potentially more phase distortion.
Should be as linear as possible over passband for minimal phase distortion. ∠H ≈ −ωtd.
Maximum deviation of |H(ω)| from unity within the passband. Smaller δp = flatter passband.

cards.front: Why can't real filters have zero transition width?
cards.back: Zero transition = rectangular |H| = sinc h(t) = non-causal. Causality forces finite transition width.
|H| = 10^(−40/20) = 0.01 = 1% of peak. Signal reduced to 1% magnitude in stopband.

cards.front: Name three practical filter design families.
cards.back: Butterworth (maximally flat), Chebyshev (equiripple, sharper), Bessel (best linear phase). Each trades off differently.
